title: 10. Inference Serving & Memory Management
subtitle: KV Cache, PagedAttention, Continuous Batching & Distributed Inference

sections.heading: Advanced Engineering for High-Throughput LLM Deployment
sections.body: The transition of LLMs from distributed training environments into high-throughput production serving exposes a fundamental divergence in hardware utilization profiles. Training frameworks are designed to optimize dense gradient calculations, maximizing GPU FLOPS via massive static matrix multiplications. Conversely, serving LLMs in production presents an entirely different computational paradigm: it is overwhelmingly **memory-bound**. [1]

sections.heading: 1. The Autoregressive Inference Bottleneck and the Memory Wall

blocks.content: At the heart of the operational bottleneck lies the **Key-Value (KV) Cache**. When a transformer generates text autoregressively, it must retain the contextual representations of the entire preceding sequence—specifically caching the Key and Value tensors for every previously processed token across every attention layer. This is mathematically necessary to avoid recomputing the entire sequence state from scratch for every newly generated token. [1]

Without a dedicated KV cache, the computational time complexity of generating a sequence of length $n$ would scale quadratically at $O(n^2)$ per single generation step. Implementing the KV cache reduces this to linear $O(n)$, but this acceleration comes at a severe spatial cost: the memory footprint grows linearly with every token added. [4]

In unoptimized implementations—such as early iterations of Hugging Face Transformers—memory is allocated contiguously and preemptively based on the model's absolute maximum context length. [1] For example, if configured for 4,096 tokens, the engine instantly reserves a contiguous VRAM block for 4,096 tokens the moment a user submits a prompt, regardless of the prompt's actual length. If the user needs only 10 tokens, the system still locks the maximum allocation.

blocks.title: The Restaurant Analogy for Memory Fragmentation
blocks.content: Imagine a restaurant where tables represent contiguous memory allocations. Reserving a massive 10-person table for a patron who only orders a coffee illustrates **internal memory fragmentation**. If ten concurrent users trigger short prompts, the memory 'restaurant' is functionally full, despite 90% of allocated space remaining entirely unutilized. [1] Prior to modern memory management, this limited a typical A100 GPU to fewer than one request per second for a 13B parameter model. [2]

This paradigm is further exacerbated by the physical limitations of modern hardware, frequently referred to as the **Memory Wall**. [8] The NVIDIA H100 GPU features approximately $3\times$ the raw compute capability (FLOPS) of the A100, driven by 4th-generation Tensor Cores and hardware FP8 support. [10] However, its memory bandwidth—upgraded from HBM2e to HBM3—experienced a significantly smaller proportional increase, from 2.0 TB/s on the A100 to 3.35 TB/s on the H100. [12]

This massive disparity between computational power growth and memory bandwidth growth dictates that autoregressive token decoding will remain perpetually bottlenecked by memory transfer speeds. [14] The silicon can perform attention calculations exponentially faster than it can physically transport model parameters and KV cache tensors from HBM into the chip's SRAM. [16] Consequently, the industry has pivoted toward specialized C++/CUDA inference engines that treat GPU VRAM like an operating system treats virtual memory, eliminating waste and allowing batch sizes to scale by orders of magnitude. [17]

sections.heading: 2.1 Attention Scaling and Statistical Variance Preservation

blocks.content: The standard scaled dot-product attention mechanism is defined as:

blocks.label: Scaled Dot-Product Attention
blocks.description: The division by √d_k is not arbitrary—it is an engineering necessity rooted in statistical variance preservation. [20]

blocks.content: Assuming query ($Q$) and key ($K$) vector components are independent random variables initialized with mean $0$ and variance $1$, the dot product of two such vectors of dimension $d_k$ is the sum of their element-wise products. Because the variance of a sum of independent random variables equals the sum of their individual variances, the expected variance of the unscaled dot product is: [22]

blocks.label: Dot Product Variance
blocks.description: The standard deviation of the dot product is exactly √d_k. [24]

blocks.content: Without the division, as $d_k$ increases, the magnitude and spread of dot products grow significantly. When these high-variance logits are passed through the **softmax** function, it exponentially amplifies the largest values, squashing the distribution into a one-hot-like vector where a single element approaches probability $1$ while all others are driven toward zero. [22]

In training, this leads to **vanishing gradients** during backpropagation. During inference, it causes extreme numerical instability and degraded output quality, as attention becomes rigid and incapable of distributing focus across multiple relevant tokens. [22] Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the pre-softmax logits back to a variance of $1$, preserving proportional relationships and ensuring stable probability distributions. [25]

While early transformers used absolute sinusoidal encodings, modern SOTA models—including LLaMA, Mistral, and DeepSeek—utilize **Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE)**. [28] RoPE achieves an efficient geometric mapping that encodes absolute positional information while preserving relative positional decay, without appending separate relative-position bias matrices. [30]

For a token embedding vector $x$ at position $m$, RoPE applies a rotation matrix. Viewing the vector components in the 2D complex plane, the operation for a single pair of dimensions uses Euler's formula:

blocks.label: RoPE Rotation
blocks.description: When the dot product between a query at position m and a key at position n is computed, the rotation vectors interact. Due to complex conjugate properties, the result inherently represents their relative distance (m − n). [32]

blocks.content: Extended to multi-dimensional embeddings, RoPE divides the $d$-dimensional vector into $d/2$ distinct pairs, applying a varying, predefined angular frequency $\theta_i$ to each pair. The resulting sparse block-diagonal matrix transformation rotates the vectors in sequence. This geometric property ensures that attention scores naturally encode the exact distance between tokens, which heavily influences how KV cache data is structured and retrieved. [32]

sections.heading: 2.3 Spatial Complexity: The KV Cache Memory Formula

blocks.content: The primary variable determining maximum permissible concurrency in an LLM deployment is the memory capacity required for the KV Cache. The total footprint can be modeled precisely, allowing engineers to calculate exact hardware requirements prior to deployment. [34]

blocks.label: KV Cache Size Formula
blocks.tex: \text{KV Cache} = 2 \times B \times S \times L \times H_{kv} \times d_h \times P
blocks.description: Where $B$ = batch size, $S$ = total sequence length, $L$ = transformer layers, $H_{kv}$ = number of KV attention heads, $d_h$ = hidden dimension per head, $P$ = bytes per parameter. The constant 2 accounts for both Key and Value tensors. [36]

blocks.title: LLaMA-2 7B Example
blocks.content: For LLaMA-2 7B in FP16, a single request with 4,096 tokens yields ~2.1 GB of KV cache. Batching 64 such requests demands **~137 GB of VRAM**—massively eclipsing the 14 GB needed for model weights alone. This drives the shift from MHA to GQA and MQA. [37]

In a GQA design, the $n_h$ query heads are clustered into $G$ groups, where each group shares a single KV head. If an architecture defines $n_h$ query heads and $n_h/G$ KV heads, the KV cache footprint shrinks by a factor of $G$. This directly translates to a $G\times$ larger maximum batch size on equivalent hardware. [38]

sections.heading: 3. Eradicating Memory Fragmentation: The PagedAttention Paradigm

blocks.content: The introduction of the **PagedAttention** algorithm by the vLLM project fundamentally altered the landscape of LLM inference. [40] By recognizing that dot-product computations within attention do not strictly require physically contiguous Key and Value arrays, the system adapts the concept of **virtual memory and demand paging** from traditional operating systems and applies it directly to GPU VRAM. [19]

Under PagedAttention, the continuous logical KV cache sequence is divided into small, fixed-size chunks called **blocks** or **pages**. [42] A single block typically stores K and V vectors for exactly 16 consecutive tokens. [43]

Because blocks are fixed-size and allocated dynamically on demand, **external memory fragmentation** is entirely eliminated. Internal fragmentation is bounded to the unfilled slots of the final block—at worst, 15 tokens of wasted memory if a sequence terminates after writing one token into a fresh 16-token block. [7]
The system uses a centralized **BlockSpaceManager** and a **BlockTable** to translate logical token positions into physical GPU memory addresses on the fly. [2]

This dynamic mapping enables a critical enterprise feature: **memory sharing via Copy-on-Write (CoW) semantics**. If multiple concurrent requests share an identical system prompt (e.g., 'You are a helpful AI assistant...'), vLLM maps their independent logical block tables to the exact same physical blocks in VRAM. Only when sequences diverge does the system duplicate the physical block, drastically reducing memory overhead for multi-turn agents and parallel sampling algorithms like beam search. [42]

blocks.title: Block Manager Architecture: Logical to Physical Mapping
blocks.code:
flowchart TD
  subgraph Request_Processing["Request Processing Layer"]
    Req1[Request 1]
    Req2[Request 2]
  end
  subgraph Logical_Mapping["Logical to Physical Mapping"]
    BT1[Block Table 1]
    BT2[Block Table 2]
  end
  subgraph Memory_Manager["Engine Core Memory Manager"]
    BM[Block Manager]
    CoW[CoW Monitor]
  end
  subgraph VRAM["Physical GPU VRAM"]
    PB1["Physical Block 1 (Shared Prefix)"]
    PB2[Physical Block 2]
    PB3[Physical Block 3]
    PB4[Physical Block 4 - Free]
  end
  Req1 --> BT1
  Req2 --> BT2
  BT1 -."Logical 0".-> PB1
  BT1 -."Logical 1".-> PB2
  BT2 -."Logical 0".-> PB1
  BT2 -."Logical 1".-> PB3
  BM -->|"Allocates/Reclaims"| PB4
  CoW -->|"Tracks refs"| PB1

blocks.content: Both requests utilize Physical Block 1 for their identical system prompts. The Block Manager tracks integer reference counts, and the CoW monitor physically duplicates the block into a new allocation only when one sequence writes divergent data into it. [46]

The engineering translation of PagedAttention requires a robust orchestration layer in Python using PyTorch. Below is a clean implementation demonstrating how to construct the BlockManager, Block, and BlockTable data structures from scratch with O(1) time complexity allocations. [46]

During model initialization, vLLM profiles memory required for model weights. Based on the `--gpu-memory-utilization` parameter (defaulting to 0.90), it seizes the remaining available VRAM and divides it into blocks, creating a massive, pre-allocated empty tensor as the KV cache pool. [1]

While the PyTorch layer handles metadata, the actual forward pass must fetch high-dimensional keys and values from non-contiguous addresses at lightning speed. vLLM achieves this via highly optimized custom **Triton or CUDA kernels** that operate directly on scattered memory. [50]

The physical cache structure is pre-allocated as two massive tensors shaped as `[num_physical_blocks, num_kv_heads, head_size, block_size]`. The low-level kernel extracts relevant physical blocks using the sequences' block tables.

blocks.code:
import triton
import triton.language as tl

# Simplified Triton pseudo-code for PagedAttention execution
@triton.jit
def paged_attention_kernel(
    query_ptr,        # shape: [num_seqs, num_heads, head_size]
    key_cache_ptr,    # shape: [num_blocks, num_kv_heads, head_size, block_size]
    value_cache_ptr,  # shape: [num_blocks, num_kv_heads, head_size, block_size]
    block_tables_ptr, # shape: [num_seqs, max_blocks_per_seq]
    context_lens_ptr, # shape: [num_seqs]
    scale,            # float32: 1 / sqrt(d_k)
    BLOCK_SIZE: tl.constexpr,
    HEAD_SIZE: tl.constexpr
):
    # Determine which sequence and attention head this instance handles
    seq_idx = triton.program_id(0)
    head_idx = triton.program_id(1)

    # Load query for current sequence and head from HBM to SRAM
    q_offset = (seq_idx * num_heads * HEAD_SIZE) + (head_idx * HEAD_SIZE)
    q = tl.load(query_ptr + q_offset + tl.arange(0, HEAD_SIZE))

    # Retrieve block table mapping and context length
    block_table_offset = seq_idx * max_blocks_per_seq
    ctx_len = tl.load(context_lens_ptr + seq_idx)
    num_logical_blocks = tl.cdiv(ctx_len, BLOCK_SIZE)

    # Initialize accumulator
    acc = tl.zeros([HEAD_SIZE], dtype=tl.float32)

    # Iterate over logical blocks, performing indirect memory reads
    for logical_idx in range(num_logical_blocks):
        # Translate logical index to physical block ID
        physical_idx = tl.load(block_tables_ptr + block_table_offset + logical_idx)
        # Load non-contiguous K and V using the physical block ID
        kv_offset = (physical_idx * num_kv_heads * HEAD_SIZE * BLOCK_SIZE)
        k = tl.load(key_cache_ptr + kv_offset + ...)
        v = tl.load(value_cache_ptr + kv_offset + ...)
        # Compute scaled dot-product attention for the chunk
        qk = tl.dot(q, k) * scale
        weights = tl.softmax(qk)
        acc += tl.dot(weights, v)

    # Write finalized attention output back to HBM
    out_offset = (seq_idx * num_heads * HEAD_SIZE) + (head_idx * HEAD_SIZE)
    tl.store(out_ptr + out_offset + tl.arange(0, HEAD_SIZE), acc)

blocks.content: Modern versions use **Persistent Kernels** where the grid size equals the total streaming multiprocessors (SMs). Instead of the CPU launching a grid proportional to batch size, a fixed number of kernel instances dynamically read work from GPU memory. This circumvents massive overhead of variable launch grids and optimizes the backend for **CUDA Graphs**, maintaining stability under volatile traffic patterns. [52]

In naive, request-level batching, the inference engine waits for all requests in a static batch to completely finish before starting a new batch. [54] Because generation lengths are wildly unpredictable, short sequences finish quickly, leaving GPU compute slots entirely idle while longer sequences continue for hundreds of iterations. [55]

vLLM utilizes **Continuous Batching** (Iteration-Level Scheduling). At the end of every single decoding iteration—representing exactly one token per sequence—the engine's scheduler re-evaluates the running queue. [53] If a sequence finishes (emits EOS), its blocks are immediately returned to the BlockSpaceManager. Simultaneously, a new request from the waiting queue is pulled into the active batch. [53] This ensures maximum, uninterrupted GPU compute saturation.

blocks.title: Scheduler State Machine
blocks.code:
stateDiagram-v2
  [*] --> Waiting: API Request Received
  Waiting --> Running: Schedule Prefill
  state Running {
    [*] --> DecodeStep
    DecodeStep --> CheckEOS
    CheckEOS --> DecodeStep: Token Generated
  }
  Running --> Swapped: Preemption - VRAM Exhausted
  Swapped --> Running: Swap-In - VRAM Available
  Running --> Preempted_Recompute: Preemption - VRAM Exhausted
  Preempted_Recompute --> Waiting: Rerun Prefill Later
  CheckEOS --> Finished: EOS Token Generated
  Finished --> [*]: Release KV Blocks

blocks.content: Requests exist within a strict state machine managed by the Scheduler. Transitions are governed by the `token_budget` and `max_num_seqs` parameters. [57]

Given the autoregressive nature of LLMs, sequences grow unpredictably and the KV cache can run out of physical blocks even with continuous batching. When `can_allocate()` returns false, the scheduler triggers **preemption** to prioritize actively decoding sequences. [58]

items.title: Swapping
items.description: Pages out KV blocks of lower-priority sequences to system CPU RAM over PCIe. Blocks are fetched back when the sequence resumes. [43]
cons: Massive overhead with small block sizes (16 tokens) due to thousands of tiny PCIe transfers. [43]

items.title: Recomputation (Default)
items.description: Completely evicts blocks, destroying the data. When resumed, a fresh prefill pass regenerates the KV cache from scratch. [58]
pros: GPU Tensor Cores process prefill extremely fast; overhead rarely exceeds 20% of PCIe swapping latency. [43]

blocks.title: Default is Recomputation
blocks.content: Counter-intuitively, vLLM's default preemption mode is **Recomputation**, not Swapping. The GPU's Tensor Cores process prefill matrix multiplications so fast that recomputation overhead practically never exceeds 20% of the latency incurred by PCIe swapping. [43]

The inference process consists of two vastly different operational profiles: the **compute-bound Prefill** stage (processing the full input prompt simultaneously) and the **memory-bandwidth-bound Decode** stage (generating outputs token-by-token). [61]

Mixing a massive 10,000-token prefill request into the same active batch as ongoing decode requests causes catastrophic latency spikes. The GPU pauses for the massive prefill matrix multiplication, heavily degrading **Inter-Token Latency (ITL)** for users currently reading their streaming responses. [63]

Modern engines employ **Chunked Prefill**. [63] Instead of processing a massive prompt in one pass, the scheduler fragments it into sized chunks (e.g., 512 tokens). [64] These chunks are co-scheduled alongside decode tokens, maintaining high GPU Tensor Core utilization from the prefill's dense linear operations without starving active decodes, flattening the latency curve. [64]

Chunking prefills introduces severe tradeoffs with **Prefix Caching**. In standard vLLM, when a prefill is chunked, only the initial chunk benefits from KV cache matching. Subsequent chunks are treated as standard running requests, bypassing the prefix cache hit logic and resulting in redundant computations. [66]

To address these inefficiencies, competing frameworks like **SGLang** use **RadixAttention**. [67] Instead of a flat dictionary of block hashes, SGLang organizes the KV cache using a **Radix tree**. [54] In scenarios involving autonomous agentic workflows, multi-shot prompting, or RAG where multiple requests share extensive prefixes, RadixAttention caches and traverses these prefixes with mathematical precision. [69]

Benchmarks demonstrate SGLang can exhibit up to a **29% throughput advantage** over fully optimized vLLM setups, proving that tree-based semantic caching offers superior resilience against memory pressure compared to linear block hashing. [69]

For the best of both worlds, enterprise-scale operations implement **Disaggregated Serving Architectures** (such as llm-d). Dedicated GPU nodes handle exclusively prefill workloads at maximum throughput. Once computed, KV cache tensors are transmitted over high-speed InfiniBand networks directly into the PagedAttention blocks of dedicated decoding GPU nodes. [71]

sections.heading: 7. Distributed Inference: Tensor, Pipeline, and Expert Parallelism

blocks.content: When a model's weight footprint exceeds a single accelerator's physical memory, distributed inference strategies must be invoked. Standard architectures leverage combinations of **Tensor Parallelism**, **Pipeline Parallelism**, and **Expert Parallelism**. [72]

vLLM implements **Tensor Parallelism (TP)** based on the Megatron-LM architecture. [74] Instead of splitting by layers (which forces sequential GPU waiting), TP shards individual matrix multiplications within a single layer across multiple GPUs simultaneously. [72]

items.label: ColumnParallelLinear
items.detail: The first MLP weight matrix ($W_1$) is sliced vertically (column-wise). Each GPU computes an independent subset of intermediate activation features. Requires zero synchronization. [76]

items.label: RowParallelLinear
items.detail: The second MLP weight matrix ($W_2$) is sliced horizontally (row-wise). Each GPU processes its local activation subset and outputs a partial sum. [76]

items.label: All-Reduce
items.detail: Aggregates and sums partial outputs across all GPUs to finalize layer output. Because All-Reduce occurs after every attention and MLP block, TP is bottlenecked by slow interconnects. [41] Therefore, TP is confined to single-node multi-GPU arrays using NVLink (up to 900 GB/s bidirectional) and GPUDirect RDMA. [41]

blocks.content: **Pipeline Parallelism (PP)** divides the model vertically by layer depth. [36] The first 20 layers execute on Node A, the next 20 on Node B. [72] PP drastically reduces network communication—only a single activation tensor crosses the network boundary. [82] However, it introduces idle 'bubbles' where GPUs wait for activations from previous stages.

For **Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)** models (DeepSeek-V3, Mixtral, Qwen), **Expert Parallelism (EP)** is introduced. [79] MoE models contain dozens of distinct MLP 'experts' but only route tokens to a few during a forward pass. EP assigns specific experts to dedicated GPUs. [84] During the forward pass, a specialized **All-To-All** routing mechanism distributes tokens to the relevant GPUs, computes, and gathers results. Combining Data Parallelism with EP enables optimal KV cache deduplication while maintaining expert utilization across massive clusters. [73]

The fundamental limitation of memory-saving variants like GQA is that aggressively reducing KV heads eventually restricts the model's expressivity and capacity to track complex semantic relationships across long contexts. [85]

DeepSeek-V2/V3 introduced **Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA)**, shifting from simple head-sharing to **dynamic low-rank compression**. [87] Instead of computing and caching high-dimensional K and V matrices per head, MLA compresses the hidden attention input $h_t$ into a single, low-dimensional latent vector $c_t$ of dimension $d_c \ll d_h$. [87]

blocks.label: MLA Down-Projection
blocks.description: This compressed latent vector $c_t$ is the only element written to the KV cache. During decoding, the model retrieves $c_t$ and regenerates full K and V on the fly using up-projection matrices $W_K^{\text{up}}$ and $W_V^{\text{up}}$. [89]

blocks.content: The additional up-projection multiplications force the GPU to execute $2\times$ to $3\times$ more scalar arithmetic than standard MHA. [90] Yet, because LLM inference is memory-bandwidth bound, this is a highly advantageous trade-off. The drastic reduction in KV cache retrieval—up to **93.3% smaller**—significantly boosts maximum generation throughput. [88] MLA fundamentally converts the bottleneck from memory bandwidth back into pure compute, utilizing previously under-saturated Tensor Cores to bypass the memory wall. [91]

At the absolute limits of hardware optimization, the bit-level precision of cached tensors is reduced. While model weights are routinely quantized to 8-bit or 4-bit, the industry is increasingly applying quantization directly to the **KV cache**. [93]

Techniques like **XQuant** process layer input activations dynamically, mapping FP16 values into structured, dense-and-sparse quantized representations. [93] On cutting-edge hardware like NVIDIA Blackwell (GB200), data types such as **NVFP4** (4-bit Floating Point) are natively supported by the hardware tensor engine. [95]

DeepSeek deployments on GB200 architectures utilize FlashInfer's custom TRTLLM-Gen GEMM kernels to dequantize packed 4-bit representations on-the-fly directly within SM registers. [96] Reducing KV cache precision from FP16 to FP8 or FP4 slashes VRAM footprint by **50% to 75%**, proportionally doubling or quadrupling the maximum sequence concurrency limit with minimal perplexity degradation. [35]

The transition from deploying models in constrained academic environments to serving them at global scale rests entirely on mastery of low-level memory operations. Standard training infrastructure, optimized for contiguous memory allocation and dense gradient calculations, structurally collapses under the inherently fragmented, sequential, autoregressive requirements of production text generation.

PagedAttention within high-performance engines functionally eradicates this issue. By adapting OS-level virtual memory mapping, breaking cache matrices into 16-token physical blocks, and managing them dynamically through logical Block Tables, AI engineers eliminated internal memory fragmentation. Integrating these with continuous iteration-level scheduling, Copy-on-Write prefix caching, and Megatron-style tensor-parallel sharding transforms the GPU from a passive matrix multiplier into a highly saturated inference server.

Yet, raw compute capabilities continue to radically outpace memory bandwidth. The future of inference engineering relies on fundamental architectural innovations—like DeepSeek's **Multi-Head Latent Attention**, **Speculative Decoding** pipelines, and **Disaggregated Prefill** routing topologies—that deliberately sacrifice massive surpluses of computational FLOPs to aggressively compress physical data movement, outmaneuvering the physical constraints of the memory wall.

points: **KV Cache** is the primary memory bottleneck in LLM serving—for LLaMA-2 7B, batching 64 requests demands ~137 GB of VRAM for cache alone.
**PagedAttention** adapts OS virtual memory paging to GPU VRAM, eliminating 60-80% memory waste from fragmentation.
**Copy-on-Write** semantics allow shared system prompts to occupy identical physical blocks, drastically reducing multi-tenant overhead.
**Continuous Batching** (Iteration-Level Scheduling) replaces static batching, ensuring maximum GPU utilization by allowing per-token entry/exit.
vLLM defaults to **Recomputation** over Swapping for preemption—GPU prefill is so fast that recompute overhead < 20% of PCIe swap latency.
**Chunked Prefill** prevents prefill jitter from degrading decode ITL for active users.
**RadixAttention** (SGLang) provides up to 29% throughput advantage over vLLM for prefix-heavy workloads via tree-based KV cache.
**MLA** (DeepSeek) achieves 93.3% KV cache reduction by trading memory bandwidth for compute via low-rank latent compression.
**KV cache quantization** (FP8/FP4/NVFP4) halves or quarters VRAM footprint with minimal quality loss on Blackwell hardware.
